title: 10 best herbal teas for jet lag

remedy_properties: Sedative and calming effects: Valerian tea's sedative and calming properties can help alleviate stress, anxiety, and restlessness caused by jet lag, promoting relaxation and calmness.

GABA-boosting properties: Valerian tea contains compounds that increase the production of GABA (Gamma-Aminobutyric Acid), a neurotransmitter that helps regulate sleep-wake cycles, making it easier to fall asleep after crossing time zones.

Adaptogenic properties: Valerian tea's adaptogenic properties allow it to help the body adjust to changes in its normal rhythms and habits, reducing the impact of jet lag on the body's natural circadian rhythms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian, teas helps with jet lag because it promotes relaxation and improves sleep quality. The herb contains valerenic acid, a natural sedative that helps regulate the body's internal clock. By calming the mind and body, valerian tea allows travelers to adjust to new time zones more efficiently, reducing fatigue and discomfort associated with jet lag. As a result, individuals can quickly recover from long-distance travel and feel refreshed and revitalized.
remedy_constituents: Valerenic acid: Acts as a GABA receptor agonist, promoting relaxation and reducing the symptoms of insomnia and fatigue associated with jet lag.
Valerenal: Exhibits sedative properties, helping to regulate the body's circadian rhythms and improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during travel across time zones.
Isovaleric acid: Has a sedative effect and can help reduce anxiety and stress related to jet lag, promoting a sense of calmness and relaxation.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are the primary part used due to their high content of valerenic acid, which is responsible for the sedative and relaxing properties of the plant.
Leaves: They also contain valerenic acid and other active compounds, making them a suitable alternative to roots for making teas.
Seeds: Some preparations use seeds due to their presence of valerenic acid and other alkaloids, although they are less commonly used than roots or leaves.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried valeriana officinalis roots from a trusted source with a clean pair of scissors.
Dry the harvested roots for 2-3 weeks in a well-ventilated area to reduce moisture content.
Chop the dried roots into small pieces using a sharp knife to increase their surface area.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of chopped roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup and discard the solids immediately.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic compounds: Herbal teas like ashwagandha, ginseng, and rhodiola rosea contain adaptogenic compounds that help your body adjust to changes in environment and routine, reducing stress and fatigue caused by jet lag.

Antioxidants: Herbal teas like green tea, peppermint, and lemon balm are rich in antioxidants that can help protect against oxidative stress and inflammation caused by traveling across time zones, promoting overall well-being and recovery.

Aromatics and calming compounds: Herbal teas like chamomile, lavender, and valerian root contain aromatics and calming compounds that can promote relaxation, reduce anxiety and restlessness, and regulate sleep patterns disrupted by jet lag.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Camellia sinensis, also known as tea, teas helps with jet lag because of its unique combination of caffeine and L-theanine. The caffeine in tea provides a gentle energy boost, while the L-theanine promotes relaxation and reduces stress levels. This balance of stimulating and calming effects helps to regulate the body's internal clock, making it easier to adjust to new time zones. As a result, tea can help alleviate the symptoms of jet lag, including fatigue, insomnia, and disorientation.
remedy_constituents: Catechins: These polyphenolic compounds help regulate the body's circadian rhythm and reduce oxidative stress caused by jet lag, promoting a faster recovery.
Theaflavins: These flavonoid compounds have been shown to improve sleep quality and duration, which is often disrupted during jet lag, by interacting with the body's natural sleep-wake cycle.
L-theanine: This amino acid has a calming effect on the mind and body, reducing stress and anxiety associated with jet lag, and promoting relaxation and improved sleep quality.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Camellia sinensis leaves are the most commonly used part to make teas for jet lag due to their high content of L-theanine, which helps regulate sleep and wake cycles.

Flowers: Camellia sinensis flowers are sometimes used to make teas for jet lag due to their ability to promote relaxation and reduce stress, which can be beneficial for travelers experiencing jet lag.
Leaves: (Although mentioned twice, Camellia sinensis leaves are indeed the most used part for teas related to jet lag due to their numerous benefits.)
remedy_recipe: Choose high-quality camellia sinensis leaves from reputable sources for optimal flavor and nutritional benefits.
Rinse the tea leaves in cold running water to remove any impurities and debris thoroughly.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of loose-leaf camellia sinensis for every 8 oz of water required.
Steep the tea leaves in boiling water for 3 to 5 minutes to release desired flavors and antioxidants.
Strain the tea leaves from the liquid using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids completely.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic and Stress-Reducing Properties: Lemon balm tea's adaptogenic properties help the body adjust to new sleep schedules, reducing stress and anxiety caused by jet lag, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep.

Sedative Effects: The sedative compounds in lemon balm tea, such as rosmarinic acid and citral, promote relaxation, calmness, and drowsiness, which can help individuals overcome the effects of jet lag-related insomnia.

Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Properties: Lemon balm tea's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce oxidative stress caused by long-distance travel, which can contribute to fatigue, irritability, and other symptoms associated with jet lag.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, teas helps with jet lag because of its adaptogenic properties and ability to regulate the body's internal clock. The tea's calming effects can help reduce stress and promote relaxation, making it easier to adjust to new time zones. Additionally, lemon balm's antioxidant properties may help alleviate fatigue and promote a sense of well-being, allowing the body to recover from the physical and mental strain of long-distance travel. This makes it a popular choice among travelers to combat jet lag.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: A phenolic compound that helps alleviate jet lag by reducing oxidative stress, inflammation, and promoting better sleep quality.
Luteolin: A flavonoid that assists in regulating the body's circadian rhythms, improving sleep quality, and reducing fatigue associated with jet lag.
Melissic acid: A triterpenoid that has been shown to have a sedative effect, helping to calm the mind and body, and promoting relaxation, which can aid in adjusting to a new time zone.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are commonly used due to their high content of essential oils, particularly linalool and linalyl acetate, which have a calming and sleep-promoting effect.
Buds: The buds are used for their floral flavor and aroma, which can help to relax the mind and body after traveling.
Leaves: The leaves are also used for their adaptogenic properties, helping the body to adjust to new time zones and reduce the effects of jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves and flowers when in full bloom to ensure optimal flavor and aroma.
Dry the harvested melissa leaves and flowers in a warm place for 7 to 10 days to preserve them.
Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried melissa flowers per 8 ounce cup of boiling water.
Steep the dried melissa flowers in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release their active compounds.
Strain the melissa tea and serve it immediately to enjoy its calming and soothing effects.

remedy_properties: Passionflower flavonoids: These compounds have been shown to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, helping to ease the stress and discomfort associated with jet lag.
Vitamin C: Maypop tea is a rich source of vitamin C, which can help boost the immune system and combat fatigue, common symptoms of jet lag.
Hesperidin: This flavonoid has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate headaches and other physical symptoms that can occur when traveling across time zones.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata, also known as maypop, teas helps with jet lag because it promotes relaxation and reduces stress, making it easier to adjust to new time zones. The herb contains flavonoids and other compounds that have a calming effect on the nervous system, helping to regulate sleep patterns and improve the quality of sleep. By promoting a restful night's sleep, Passiflora incarnata tea can alleviate jet lag symptoms, allowing travelers to feel more refreshed and prepared to tackle their next destination.
remedy_constituents: Harmane: Harmane, an alkaloid compound in Passiflora incarnata, has been shown to help regulate sleep patterns, which can be disrupted by jet lag.
Isorhapontigenin: Isorhapontigenin, a stilbenoid phenolic compound, has been found to have antioxidant properties and can help reduce inflammation caused by jet lag-related stress.
Hernandezioside: Hernandezioside, a saponin glycoside, has been reported to have sedative effects and can help reduce symptoms of insomnia and sleep disorders associated with jet lag.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are the most commonly used part for making teas due to their high concentration of flavonoids and alkaloids that help with relaxation and stress relief.
Fruits: Fruits are used to make teas due to their rich content of vitamin C and antioxidants that help in reducing fatigue and promoting a good night's sleep.
Roots: Roots are used for their sedative properties, which help in calming the mind and body, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried passiflora incarnata flowers in late summer or early fall season.
Clean the harvested flowers by gently rinsing them with filtered water to remove impurities.
Steep one teaspoon of dried flowers or three teaspoons of fresh flowers in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids and discard.
Store the cooled herbal tea in a clean glass container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedy_properties: Anxiolytic properties: Chamomile tea helps to reduce anxiety and stress associated with jet lag, allowing for better sleep and relaxation.

Sedative effects: The apigenin and luteolin compounds in chamomile tea have a sedative effect, promoting relaxation and reducing symptoms of insomnia and restlessness caused by travel across time zones.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Chamomile tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help to reduce fatigue and discomfort caused by jet lag, as well as alleviate any underlying inflammation or stress that may exacerbate symptoms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Matricaria chamomilla, also known as chamomile, teas helps with jet lag because of its calming and relaxing properties. The tea's soothing effects help regulate the body's internal clock, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone. Chamomile's apigenin content interacts with the body's GABA receptors, promoting a sense of calmness and reducing stress. This, in turn, can help alleviate jet lag symptoms such as fatigue, insomnia, and anxiety, making it easier to recover from long-distance travel.
remedy_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that helps with jet lag by promoting relaxation and reducing stress through its sedative and anxiolytic properties, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.
α-Bisabolol: A sesquiterpene alcohol that contributes to the anti-inflammatory and soothing effects of chamomile tea, which can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag such as fatigue and irritability.

Luteolin: A flavonoid that exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help mitigate the negative effects of jet lag, such as oxidative stress and inflammation, and promote a smooth recovery.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in apigenin and luteolin, which have a calming effect on the body, helping to alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
Leaves: Contain apigenin and other flavonoids that promote relaxation and reduce stress caused by time zone changes.
Seeds: Rich in chamazulene, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the digestive issues often associated with jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh matricaria chamomilla flowers in the early morning or late afternoon when the petals are fully open.
Dry the harvested flowers either by air-drying or using a dehydrator at 95 degrees fahrenheit for 6 hours.
Measure 1 to 2 teaspoons of dried matricaria chamomilla flowers and place them in a tea infuser.
Steep the flowers in boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes to release their active properties and flavors.
Strain the tea and serve immediately, or let it cool and refrigerate it for later consumption.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic properties: Licorice root's adaptogenic properties help the body adjust to stress and fatigue caused by travel, promoting resilience and reducing symptoms of jet lag such as insomnia and fatigue.
Glycyrrhizin content: The glycyrrhizin in licorice root has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag like headaches, muscle aches, and digestive issues.

Aldosterone regulation: Licorice root's ability to regulate aldosterone levels can help regulate sleep-wake cycles by promoting relaxation and reducing cortisol levels, making it easier to adjust to new time zones.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with jet lag because of its adaptogenic properties that help the body adjust to new time zones. The tea's anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compounds work to reduce stress and promote relaxation, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep in a new environment. Additionally, Glycyrrhiza glabra's ability to regulate cortisol levels helps the body synchronize with the new circadian rhythms, reducing the negative effects of jet lag and promoting a smoother transition to a new time zone.
remedy_constituents: Licoricidin: Licoricidin, a triterpenoid saponin, helps with jet lag by regulating the body's internal clock and reducing oxidative stress caused by time zone changes.
Glycyrrhizin: Glycyrrhizin, a triterpenoid saponin, may help alleviate jet lag symptoms by reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy balance of cortisol and melatonin levels in the body.

Licorice flavonoids: Licorice flavonoids, particularly glabridin and licochalcone A, may aid in jet lag recovery by protecting against oxidative damage, stabilizing the body's circadian rhythms, and regulating the sleep-wake cycle.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are used to make teas for jet lag due to their anti-inflammatory properties, which help alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
Leaves: The leaves are used to make teas for jet lag as they contain antioxidants that aid in reducing fatigue and improving sleep quality.
Barks: The barks are used to make teas for jet lag due to their adaptogenic properties, which help the body adapt to changes in time zones and reduce stress.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 pounds of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots from a trusted supplier or a local herbalist.
Clean the roots by rinsing them in cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Chop the roots into small pieces using a sharp knife or a root cutter.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of the chopped roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard the solids to obtain a clear tea ready for consumption.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic compounds: Skullcap contains adaptogenic compounds like baicalin, which helps to regulate the body's natural circadian rhythms and reduce stress, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.

Antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties: The antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds in skullcap tea may help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress caused by jet lag, promoting overall well-being and reducing fatigue.

GABA-boosting effects: Skullcap has been shown to increase the levels of GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid) in the brain, which can help to promote relaxation, reduce anxiety and insomnia, and improve sleep quality, all of which are common symptoms of jet lag.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Scutellaria baicalensis, also known as skullcap, teas helps with jet lag because of its unique properties that regulate the body's internal clock. The herbal tea contains baicalein and baicalin, compounds that have been shown to improve sleep quality and duration. By promoting relaxation and reducing stress, Scutellaria baicalensis teas help to alleviate the symptoms of jet lag, such as fatigue, insomnia, and disorientation. This natural remedy also helps to adjust the body's circadian rhythms, making it easier to adapt to new time zones.
remedy_constituents: Baicalein: This flavonoid acts as a potent antioxidant, helping to reduce oxidative stress caused by jet lag, which can disrupt the body's natural rhythms.
Baicalin: A flavonoid glycoside, baicalin exhibits sedative and anti-inflammatory properties, which can aid in reducing symptoms of jet lag, such as insomnia and fatigue.

Wogonin: A flavone with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, wogonin may help regulate the body's circadian rhythms and alleviate jet lag symptoms by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress.
remedy_parts: Roots: Used due to their rich content of baicalein and baicalin, which have been shown to have adaptogenic properties that can help regulate the body's internal clock.
Leaves: Utilized for their high concentration of flavonoids, such as baicalein and baicalin, which may aid in reducing stress and promoting relaxation, ultimately helping to combat jet lag.
Flowers: Employed for their soothing and calming effects, which may help to regulate the body's circadian rhythms and alleviate symptoms associated with jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 grams of dried scutellaria baicalensis roots from a reputable source.
Measure 250-500 milliliters of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the scutellaria baicalensis roots in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a separate container.
Allow the tea to cool to room temperature before consumption.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic properties: Chinese Date Tea helps the body adapt to changes in environment, stress, and sleep patterns, making it easier to adjust to new time zones and reduce symptoms of jet lag.

Digestive benefits: The tea's soothing effects on the digestive system can help alleviate symptoms such as nausea, bloating, and stomach upset often associated with jet lag, allowing for a smoother transition to local cuisine and reducing discomfort.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties: Chinese Date Tea's rich antioxidant content helps combat oxidative stress caused by long-haul flights, while its anti-inflammatory properties may reduce fatigue, muscle soreness, and joint pain commonly experienced during and after travel.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ziziphus jujuba, also known as Chinese date, teas helps with jet lag because it contains antioxidants and flavonoids that promote relaxation and reduce stress. The tea's soothing properties can help regulate the body's internal clock, adjusting to the new time zone more efficiently. Additionally, Ziziphus jujuba's adaptogenic properties allow the body to adapt to changing environments, making it easier to overcome the effects of jet lag and feel more refreshed and alert during travel. This natural remedy can be a valuable tool for travelers.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: Flavonoids present in Ziziphus jujuba teas may help mitigate jet lag by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress, which can disrupt the body's natural circadian rhythms.
Saponins: Saponins, a type of triterpene glycoside, may help with jet lag by modulating the body's response to stress and promoting a sense of balance and well-being.

Terpenoids: Terpenoids, including compounds like quercetin and isorhapontigenin, may contribute to the adaptogenic effects of Ziziphus jujuba teas, helping the body adapt to changes in time zones and reducing the severity of jet lag symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Their tea is used to help alleviate stress and fatigue caused by jet lag, promoting relaxation and reducing travel-related anxiety.
Fruits: Jujube fruit tea is consumed to help regulate sleep patterns and improve the quality of sleep, which can be disrupted by jet lag.
Seeds: Jujube seed tea is used to help reduce stress and promote a sense of calmness, which can aid in recovering from jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh ziziphus jujuba leaves and fruits in the morning after the dew has dried completely.
Rinse the leaves and fruits with pure water to remove any dirt or debris present.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried ziziphus jujuba leaves in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Drink the tea hot or cold within 30 minutes to 1 hour of preparation.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic compounds: Cucumber tea contains adaptogens like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help your body adjust to new environments and rhythms, reducing stress and fatigue associated with jet lag.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Cucumber's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as fisetin, can help reduce inflammation caused by changes in altitude, temperature, and humidity, making it easier to adjust to new surroundings.

Hydrating effects: The high water content of cucumber tea helps replenish lost fluids and electrolytes during travel, promoting hydration and reducing the severity of jet lag symptoms like headaches, fatigue, and dry mouth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cucumis sativus, also known as cucumber, teas helps with jet lag because it contains flavonoids and phenolic compounds that have been shown to regulate the body's circadian rhythms. The tea's antioxidant properties also help to mitigate the negative effects of traveling across multiple time zones, such as fatigue and disorientation. Additionally, Cucumis sativus tea has been found to increase the production of melatonin, a hormone that regulates sleep-wake cycles, thereby promoting a smoother transition to a new time zone.
remedy_constituents: Fatty Acids: Fatty acids in Cucumis sativus teas may help regulate the body's circadian rhythms by providing a natural source of energy and supporting the production of melatonin, a hormone that helps control sleep-wake cycles.

Phenolic Acids: Phenolic acids in Cucumis sativus teas have antioxidant properties that may help reduce oxidative stress caused by jet lag, promoting relaxation and reducing inflammation associated with disrupted sleep patterns.

Triterpenoids (specifically Cucurbitacins): Triterpenoids, such as Cucurbitacins, may help regulate the body's internal clock by influencing the expression of genes involved in circadian rhythm regulation, thus aiding in the adaptation to new time zones.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used for their antioxidant and adaptogenic properties to help the body adjust to new time zones.
Seeds: Used for their melatonin-boosting properties, which can help regulate sleep patterns during jet lag.
Flowers: Used for their calming and relaxing effects, which can help reduce stress and promote better sleep quality during travel.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 5-7 fresh cucumis sativus leaves and young stems in the early morning for optimal flavor.
Rinse the harvested cucumis sativus leaves and young stems with filtered water to remove dirt.
Dry the cucumis sativus leaves and young stems in a single layer at room temperature for 7-10 days.
Steep one teaspoon of dried cucumis sativus leaves in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool before serving to enjoy the refreshing flavor.

remedy_properties: Adaptogenic properties: Wild rose tea helps your body adapt to new environments and rhythms, reducing stress and fatigue caused by jet lag. This allows you to better adjust to new time zones and schedules.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties: The antioxidants in wild rose tea help protect the body from oxidative stress and inflammation caused by travel and time changes, which can exacerbate symptoms of jet lag like headaches and insomnia.

Gastrointestinal soothing properties: Wild rose tea's gentle, anti-inflammatory effects on the digestive system can help alleviate nausea, stomach upset, and diarrhea that often accompany jet lag, allowing you to feel more comfortable and relaxed during travel.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosa laxa, also known as wild rose, teas helps with jet lag because it contains flavonoids and phenolic acids that regulate the body's circadian rhythms. The tea's adaptogenic properties allow it to adjust to the body's changing needs, reducing the disruption caused by time zone changes. Additionally, the tea's antioxidants help to combat oxidative stress and fatigue, promoting a smoother transition to a new sleep schedule. This natural remedy can aid in readjusting the body's internal clock, minimizing the effects of jet lag.
remedy_constituents: Phenolic acids: These help to regulate the body's circadian rhythms by influencing the expression of genes involved in sleep-wake cycles.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids like quercetin and kaempferol have antioxidant properties that can help alleviate fatigue and stress caused by jet lag, promoting relaxation and sleep.

Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound can help regulate the body's internal clock by influencing the expression of genes involved in circadian rhythm regulation, potentially reducing the effects of jet lag.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used to make teas that help to reduce stress and promote relaxation, which can be beneficial for jet lag.
Flowers: They are used to make teas that have anti-inflammatory properties and can help to promote a restful sleep, which can aid in adjusting to a new time zone.
Buds: They are used to make teas that have a calming effect and can help to regulate the body's circadian rhythms, which can be disrupted by jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 part of dried rosa laxa flowers and leaves in late summer when the plant is fully mature.
Dry the harvested material in a warm oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours or until crisp.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried rosa laxa flowers and leaves with 1 cup of boiling water to steep.
Allow the tea to steep for 5 to 10 minutes to release the active compounds and flavors.
Strain the tea and serve it hot or chilled depending on individual preference immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Bitter orange teas helps with jet lag because its unique blend of citrus flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins can regulate the body's internal clock and reduce the symptoms of fatigue, stress, and anxiety often associated with long-distance travel. The tea's stimulating properties can also help increase alertness and energy levels, making it easier to adapt to new time zones. Additionally, bitter orange teas' calming effects can promote a restful night's sleep, helping the body adjust to its new circadian rhythm more quickly.
remedy_properties: Citrus Limonoids: These compounds, particularly limonin and nomilinic acid, have been shown to help regulate circadian rhythms and improve sleep quality by reducing stress and anxiety. This can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag such as insomnia and disorientation.

Flavonoids: The flavonoid content in bitter orange tea has been found to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce fatigue and mental fogginess associated with jet lag, promoting a sense of clarity and focus.

Vitamin C: Bitter orange tea is a rich source of vitamin C, an essential nutrient that plays a crucial role in the regulation of cortisol levels and melatonin production. This can help regulate the body's natural sleep-wake cycle and improve the overall quality of sleep.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used to calm the body and mind, helping to alleviate symptoms of insomnia and restlessness caused by jet lag.
Seeds: The seeds contain a mild sedative compound that can help regulate the body's circadian rhythms and promote relaxation.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried citrus peel into a heat-resistant cup or infuser.
Add boiling water to the cup and steep for exactly 7 minutes to release oils.
Strain the tea leaves from the liquid using a spoon or infuser and discard solids.
Mix in one tablespoon of honey and stir until dissolved to enhance flavor.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before drinking within 15-20 minutes of waking.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Purple coneflower tea contains antioxidants that can help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress caused by long-distance travel, which can contribute to fatigue, headaches, and other symptoms associated with jet lag.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in purple coneflower tea may help alleviate symptoms such as joint pain, stiffness, and soreness that can occur during jet lag due to changes in temperature, humidity, and pressure.

Adaptogenic properties: Purple coneflower is considered an adaptogen, meaning it can help the body adapt to stressors like travel-related disruptions to the body's natural rhythms, which can lead to improved sleep quality, reduced fatigue, and enhanced mental clarity.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the purple coneflower have been traditionally used to promote relaxation and reduce stress, making them well-suited for calming the effects of jet lag.
Barks: The barks of the purple coneflower contain compounds that can help regulate sleep patterns and improve the body's natural circadian rhythms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality echinacea purpurea roots and flowers from a reputable source.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm dark place for 1-2 weeks.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried echinacea root and 1 teaspoon of dried echinacea flowers in a tea infuser.
Steep the mixture in boiling water for 5-7 minutes then strain and discard solids.
Drink 1 cup of the cooled tea twice daily starting 2 days prior to travel and continuing for 3 days after.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple coneflower teas helps with jet lag because it contains a unique blend of herbs that work together to regulate the body's natural circadian rhythms. The calming effects of the tea help reduce stress and anxiety, while its adaptogenic properties allow it to gently adjust the body's internal clock to the new time zone. Additionally, the tea's antioxidant-rich properties help to reduce inflammation and promote a sense of relaxation, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep during long flights or when adjusting to a new time zone.

St John's wort teas helps with jet lag because it contains hyperforin, a compound that regulates the body's circadian rhythms. As we travel across time zones, our internal clocks can become disrupted, leading to fatigue, insomnia, and other symptoms of jet lag. The adaptogenic properties of St John's wort allow it to help reset our natural rhythms, reducing the stress and discomfort associated with traveling. By promoting relaxation and improving sleep quality, St John's wort teas can help alleviate the effects of jet lag, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.
remedy_properties: Adaptogenic properties: St. John's Wort helps your body adapt to stress, including time zone changes, by regulating cortisol levels and promoting relaxation, which can improve sleep quality.

Antioxidant effects: The antioxidant compounds in St. John's Wort tea, such as hypericin and flavonoids, help protect the body from oxidative stress caused by jet lag, which can reduce inflammation and fatigue.

GABA-boosting properties: St. John's Wort has been shown to increase levels of GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid), a neurotransmitter that promotes relaxation and reduces anxiety, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep despite jet lag.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and bioactive compounds, the roots help regulate sleep patterns and reduce fatigue caused by jet lag.
Leaves: The leaves contain hyperforin, a compound that helps adjust the body's internal clock and promotes relaxation, essential for overcoming travel-related fatigue.
Flowers: Packed with flavonoids and hypericins, the flowers support the body's natural circadian rhythms, making it easier to adapt to new time zones.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers in early morning or afternoon when they are most potent.
Dry the harvested hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers using air circulation or low heat method for 24 hours.
Measure one teaspoon of dried hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers per cup of boiling water and steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean cup discarding solids and let it cool down to desired temperature.
Consume the tea 1-2 times daily, ideally 30 minutes before bedtime or as needed, and store any leftover in refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree teas helps with jet lag because its unique blend of antioxidants, flavonoids, and other phytochemicals has been shown to regulate circadian rhythms and improve sleep quality. The tea's calming properties can help ease the stress and anxiety that often accompanies travel, while its adaptogenic compounds aid in adjusting to new time zones by supporting the body's natural sleep-wake cycle. As a result, maidenhair tree teas can help mitigate the symptoms of jet lag, promoting a smoother transition to your destination.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Ginkgo biloba's antioxidants help protect against free radical damage caused by air travel, which can exacerbate fatigue and stress associated with jet lag.

Vasodilation and improved blood circulation: The tea's flavonoids and terpenoids dilate blood vessels and improve circulation, which can help alleviate headaches and fatigue often experienced during jet lag.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Ginkgo biloba's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation and swelling caused by changes in air pressure and humidity during travel, which can contribute to discomfort and fatigue.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the maidenhair tree contain compounds that help regulate body rhythms and alleviate symptoms of jet lag.
Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes from the maidenhair tree have been traditionally used to calm the mind and body, making them a suitable ingredient in teas for jet lag.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried ginkgo biloba leaves and one cup of boiling water.
Steep the ginkgo biloba leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes to allow for optimal extraction.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Add honey to taste if desired and stir well to combine with the herbal infusion.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before serving and consuming within 30 minutes of preparation.
